frontier.hypothesis: Cross-function forensics: instrument saEft01Init with the same BB2_QTY_DEBUG/BB2_SCHED_DEBUG/BB2_ALLOC_DEBUG/BB2_RANK_DEBUG flags used on csmd4 in s6/s25; compare the p107/p113 qty numbers in saEft01Init's h5-equivalent debug_printf window against csmd4's h5 baseline. If p107/p113 birth-death/refs match numerically, saEft01Init's 15-pin family reproduces the SAME p106/p107 LAUNCH pair-tie mechanism — meaning the pin direction saEft01Init uses IS a viable csmd4 lever direction if reproducible in pure C.
frontier.mechanism: sched.c::rank_for_schedule + local-alloc.c qty_compare determinism means matching qty tables across two functions imply matching allocation decisions. If saEft01Init's pins force a specific p106/p107 outcome that csmd4's h5 residual matches modulo pin-strength, the CATEGORY of intervention that closes saEft01Init informs the CATEGORY that would close csmd4 in pure C.
frontier.next_probe: Forensics session (NOT rederive): apply saEft01Init's candidate C body to src/system.c; rebuild instrumented cc1 (BB2_*_DEBUG=1 -da); dump saEft01Init's block=3 (debug_printf window) SCHEDDBG/QTYDBG entries; cross-reference against tmp/grind/cpu_side_move_dir_4/s6/csmd4_only.* for structural congruence at insn IDs equivalent to csmd4's 111/121 LAUNCH-tie.

frontier.hypothesis: Speculative: some pure-C form outside the h5-basin structural axis (e.g. moving the ENTIRE inline debug_printf block into a static helper function called from cpu_side_move_dir_4's do_timeout arm) presents a different RTL context to the mult-expander that avoids p106's LAUNCH birth entirely, bypassing s7's expmed.c NULL_RTX wall by never reaching that expand path.
frontier.mechanism: A separate function call would put the arg5/t0 chains in a DIFFERENT function's RTL, with fresh flow.c reg_n_sets. cpu_side_move_dir_4 becomes a wrapper that calls a helper doing the printf. This restructures the ENTIRE compilation unit for that block — a fundamentally different axis than block-local structural sweeps. Byte-neutrality risk: the target bytes are IN cpu_side_move_dir_4, so any code moved to a helper would need to inline back at maspsx/link time (impossible) — this angle is likely a cheat by shape.
frontier.next_probe: Static-analysis session (NOT rederive): read tools/gcc-2.7.2/tree-inline.c to determine whether static-inline functions with -O2 are guaranteed to inline back at cc1 time (before RTL emission). If yes, the helper's C shape could produce the target bytes without cheating. If no (or if inlining is preserved as an ABI-visible boundary), the angle is closed by construction.
